title: Husband Refuses to Pay Maintenance After Separation
summary: Wife seeks monthly maintenance from husband after separation.
scenario: A woman has been living separately from her husband for 8 months after he abandoned her without reason. She has no income and two school-going children. Her husband refuses to pay any maintenance claiming she left voluntarily.
legal_guidance: Under Section 125 CrPC, a wife and minor children are entitled to maintenance from the husband if he has sufficient means and neglects to maintain them. The court can order monthly maintenance. The wife can also file under Section 24 of the Hindu Marriage Act for interim maintenance during divorce proceedings.
relevant_law: CrPC Section 125, Hindu Marriage Act Section 24, Hindu Adoption and Maintenance Act 1956
recommended_action: 1. Consult a family lawyer immediately. 2. File a maintenance petition under Section 125 CrPC at the nearest Magistrate court. 3. Gather proof of marriage, husband's income, and children's expenses. 4. Apply for interim maintenance while the case is pending.

title: Divorce Due to Mental Cruelty
summary: Husband seeks divorce citing continuous mental harassment by wife.
scenario: A man has been married for 6 years. His wife constantly humiliates him in front of family, makes false allegations, and refuses to cohabit. He has tried counselling but the situation has not improved. He wants to file for divorce.
legal_guidance: Under the Hindu Marriage Act 1955, mental cruelty is a valid ground for divorce under Section 13(1)(ia). The petitioner must prove a pattern of behaviour that makes it impossible to live together. False allegations and persistent humiliation qualify as mental cruelty as held by the Supreme Court.
relevant_law: Hindu Marriage Act Section 13(1)(ia), IPC Section 499 (defamation if false allegations made publicly)
recommended_action: 1. Maintain a written diary of incidents with dates. 2. Collect evidence — witnesses, messages, emails. 3. File divorce petition in Family Court. 4. Apply for interim relief if needed. 5. Attend mandatory counselling sessions ordered by court.

title: Child Custody Dispute After Divorce
summary: Mother and father both claim custody of minor child after divorce.
scenario: A couple divorced 1 year ago. They have a 5-year-old daughter. The father has remarried and wants full custody. The mother, who is the primary caregiver, fears losing custody. The child currently lives with the mother.
legal_guidance: Under the Guardians and Wards Act 1890 and Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act 1956, the welfare of the child is the paramount consideration. Courts generally favour the mother for children below 5 years. The father can get visitation rights. Either parent can file a custody petition in Family Court.
relevant_law: Guardians and Wards Act 1890, Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act 1956 Section 6
recommended_action: 1. File a custody petition in Family Court. 2. Demonstrate your role as primary caregiver with evidence. 3. Show stable home environment, school records, medical care. 4. Request interim custody order. 5. Cooperate with court-appointed counsellor.

title: Husband Denies Paternity of Child
summary: Husband denies being the father of the child and refuses maintenance.
scenario: A woman's husband has denied paternity of their 2-year-old child and stopped paying maintenance. He claims the child is not his and has filed for divorce. The woman wants to establish paternity and secure maintenance for the child.
legal_guidance: The court can order a DNA test to establish paternity under Section 112 of the Indian Evidence Act. Once paternity is established, the father is legally obligated to pay maintenance under Section 125 CrPC. Denial of paternity without proof is not a valid defence.
relevant_law: Indian Evidence Act Section 112, CrPC Section 125, Hindu Marriage Act
recommended_action: 1. File a maintenance petition under CrPC 125. 2. Request the court to order a DNA paternity test. 3. Gather birth certificate and hospital records. 4. Oppose divorce petition if filed without valid grounds. 5. Consult a family lawyer.

title: Mutual Consent Divorce Process
summary: Couple wants to divorce by mutual consent but unsure of the process.
scenario: A couple married for 4 years has mutually decided to separate. They have no children and have already divided their assets informally. They want to know the fastest legal way to get divorced without a contested case.
legal_guidance: Under Section 13B of the Hindu Marriage Act, a couple can file for divorce by mutual consent after living separately for at least 1 year. The process involves two motions — first motion and second motion (after 6 months cooling period, which can be waived by the court). It is the fastest form of divorce in India.
relevant_law: Hindu Marriage Act Section 13B
recommended_action: 1. Both parties sign a joint divorce petition. 2. File in Family Court with proof of 1-year separation. 3. Attend first motion hearing. 4. Wait 6 months (or apply for waiver). 5. Attend second motion hearing for final decree.

title: Husband and In-Laws Demand Dowry
summary: Newly married woman faces dowry demands and harassment from in-laws.
scenario: A woman married 8 months ago is being harassed by her husband and in-laws for additional dowry. They demand a car and cash. She has been physically assaulted twice and is afraid to return home. She is currently staying at her parents' house.
legal_guidance: Dowry demand is a criminal offence under Section 498A IPC and Dowry Prohibition Act 1961. Physical assault is punishable under IPC. The woman can also seek protection under the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005, which provides for protection orders, residence orders, and monetary relief.
relevant_law: IPC Section 498A, Dowry Prohibition Act 1961, Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005, IPC Section 323 (assault)
recommended_action: 1. File an FIR at the nearest police station under IPC 498A. 2. Apply for a protection order under DV Act 2005 at the Magistrate court. 3. Contact the National Women Helpline: 181. 4. Preserve evidence — messages, medical reports, witnesses. 5. Consult a lawyer for both criminal and civil remedies.

title: Husband Threatens Wife with Divorce for More Dowry
summary: Husband threatens divorce unless wife brings more money from parents.
scenario: A woman's husband threatens to divorce her and send her back unless her parents give him ₹5 lakhs. He has stopped giving her household money and has taken away her phone. She is isolated and scared.
legal_guidance: This constitutes both dowry harassment (IPC 498A) and domestic violence (economic abuse under DV Act 2005). Economic abuse — withholding money, controlling finances — is explicitly covered under the DV Act. The woman can seek monetary relief, residence rights, and protection orders.
relevant_law: IPC Section 498A, DV Act 2005 Section 3 (economic abuse), Dowry Prohibition Act 1961
recommended_action: 1. Reach out to a trusted family member or NGO. 2. Call Women Helpline 181 or police helpline 100. 3. File a DV complaint with the Protection Officer in your district. 4. Apply for emergency monetary relief from the Magistrate. 5. File FIR under IPC 498A.

title: Husband Physically Abuses Wife Regularly
summary: Wife seeks legal protection from physically abusive husband.
scenario: A woman has been physically abused by her husband for 3 years. She has medical records of injuries. Her husband is a government employee. She wants to leave but fears for her safety and has two children.
legal_guidance: The DV Act 2005 provides comprehensive protection including protection orders (restraining the abuser), residence orders (right to stay in matrimonial home), custody orders for children, and compensation. A criminal FIR can also be filed under IPC 323/324. The husband's government job can be affected by a conviction.
relevant_law: DV Act 2005, IPC Section 323 (voluntarily causing hurt), IPC Section 324 (hurt by dangerous weapon), IPC Section 498A
recommended_action: 1. Preserve all medical records as evidence. 2. Contact the District Protection Officer (DPO) for DV complaint. 3. File FIR at police station. 4. Apply for protection order and residence order in Magistrate court. 5. Seek custody of children simultaneously.

title: False Dowry Case Filed Against Husband's Family
summary: Husband's family falsely accused of dowry harassment seeks legal remedy.
scenario: A man's wife filed an FIR under IPC 498A against him, his parents, and his sister after a matrimonial dispute. The allegations are false — there was no dowry demand. The entire family including elderly parents has been named. They want to know how to defend themselves.
legal_guidance: The Supreme Court in Rajesh Sharma v. State of UP (2017) issued guidelines to prevent misuse of Section 498A. Anticipatory bail can be sought immediately. The accused can file a counter-complaint if the allegations are demonstrably false. A quashing petition can be filed in the High Court if the FIR is malicious.
relevant_law: IPC Section 498A, CrPC Section 438 (anticipatory bail), CrPC Section 482 (quashing of FIR)
recommended_action: 1. Immediately apply for anticipatory bail under CrPC 438. 2. Engage a criminal lawyer. 3. Gather evidence disproving allegations — bank records, witnesses, messages. 4. File for quashing of FIR in High Court if allegations are false. 5. File counter-complaint for false FIR if evidence supports it.

title: Husband Abandons Wife After Taking Dowry
summary: Husband takes dowry and abandons wife within months of marriage.
scenario: A woman's husband took ₹8 lakhs in cash and jewellery as dowry at the time of marriage. Within 3 months he abandoned her and stopped all contact. He is now living with another woman. The wife wants her dowry back and legal action against him.
legal_guidance: The husband can be prosecuted under the Dowry Prohibition Act 1961 for taking dowry. The wife can file for return of dowry (Streedhan) as a civil claim. She can also file for divorce on grounds of desertion (2 years) and cruelty. A criminal complaint under IPC 406 (criminal breach of trust) can be filed for Streedhan.
relevant_law: Dowry Prohibition Act 1961, IPC Section 406 (criminal breach of trust for Streedhan), IPC Section 498A, Hindu Marriage Act Section 13 (desertion)
recommended_action: 1. File FIR under IPC 406 and 498A for dowry and abandonment. 2. File civil suit for recovery of Streedhan. 3. File divorce petition on grounds of desertion and cruelty. 4. Claim maintenance under CrPC 125. 5. Document all dowry items with receipts, photos, witnesses.

title: Defective Mobile Phone Not Replaced by Company
summary: Consumer seeks replacement for defective phone under warranty.
scenario: A person bought a smartphone worth ₹25,000 three months ago. The phone developed a screen defect within 2 months. The company's service centre repaired it twice but the problem persists. The company is now refusing to replace or refund the phone.
legal_guidance: Under the Consumer Protection Act 2019, a consumer can file a complaint for defective goods. If the defect persists after repair, the consumer is entitled to replacement or refund. Complaints up to ₹1 crore go to the District Consumer Commission. The company can also be held liable for deficiency in service.
relevant_law: Consumer Protection Act 2019, Section 2(10) (defect), Section 35 (complaint to District Commission)
recommended_action: 1. Send a legal notice to the company demanding replacement/refund within 15 days. 2. If no response, file complaint at District Consumer Commission. 3. Attach purchase invoice, warranty card, repair records. 4. Claim compensation for mental harassment. 5. Filing fee is nominal (₹100–₹500).

title: Online Shopping — Product Not Delivered, No Refund
summary: E-commerce platform refuses refund after non-delivery of paid order.
scenario: A person ordered a laptop worth ₹45,000 on an e-commerce platform. The order was marked as delivered but never received. The platform's customer service is unresponsive and has denied the refund claim after 45 days.
legal_guidance: This is a clear case of deficiency in service under the Consumer Protection Act 2019. E-commerce platforms are covered under the Consumer Protection (E-Commerce) Rules 2020. The consumer can file a complaint with the National Consumer Helpline (1800-11-4000) or directly at the District Consumer Commission.
relevant_law: Consumer Protection Act 2019, Consumer Protection (E-Commerce) Rules 2020, IT Act 2000
recommended_action: 1. File complaint on National Consumer Helpline portal (consumerhelpline.gov.in). 2. Send legal notice to the platform's registered address. 3. File complaint at District Consumer Commission with order details, payment proof, delivery status screenshot. 4. Claim full refund + compensation for harassment.

title: Insurance Claim Wrongfully Rejected
summary: Health insurance claim rejected without valid reason by insurer.
scenario: A person was hospitalised for 10 days and incurred ₹1.8 lakh in medical expenses. His health insurance company rejected the claim citing a pre-existing condition that was never disclosed at the time of policy purchase. The person had no knowledge of this condition.
legal_guidance: Insurance companies cannot reject claims on grounds of pre-existing conditions if the policyholder was not aware of them at the time of purchase. The Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI) has guidelines on this. The consumer can file a complaint with the Insurance Ombudsman or the Consumer Commission.
relevant_law: Consumer Protection Act 2019, Insurance Act 1938, IRDAI Guidelines on Health Insurance
recommended_action: 1. Send a written complaint to the insurance company's grievance cell. 2. If unresolved in 30 days, file complaint with Insurance Ombudsman (free of cost). 3. Alternatively file at District Consumer Commission. 4. Attach policy document, hospitalisation records, rejection letter. 5. Claim full amount + interest + compensation.

title: Builder Charges Extra for Amenities Not Delivered
summary: Builder collects money for club house and amenities but never builds them.
scenario: A flat buyer paid ₹3 lakhs extra for promised amenities including a clubhouse, swimming pool, and gym. After possession, none of these amenities exist. The builder says they are 'under construction' even 3 years after possession.
legal_guidance: This is both a consumer complaint and a RERA violation. The builder is liable for deficiency in service under the Consumer Protection Act 2019. Under RERA, the builder must deliver all promised amenities. The buyer can file complaints in both forums simultaneously.
relevant_law: Consumer Protection Act 2019, RERA Section 18, RERA Section 11 (promoter obligations)
recommended_action: 1. File complaint with State RERA Authority for non-delivery of amenities. 2. File consumer complaint at District Consumer Commission. 3. Attach brochure, sale agreement showing promised amenities, payment receipts. 4. Claim refund of extra amount paid + compensation. 5. Join with other flat buyers for a stronger case.

title: Electricity Bill Overcharged by DISCOM
summary: Consumer receives abnormally high electricity bill and seeks correction.
scenario: A household received an electricity bill of ₹18,000 for a month when their average bill is ₹1,200. The meter reading appears incorrect. The electricity board is insisting on payment and threatening disconnection.
legal_guidance: Under the Electricity Act 2003, consumers have the right to challenge incorrect bills. The consumer can file a complaint with the Consumer Grievance Redressal Forum (CGRF) of the electricity distribution company. If unresolved, the Electricity Ombudsman can be approached. Disconnection cannot happen while a complaint is pending.
relevant_law: Electricity Act 2003, Section 42 (consumer rights), Consumer Protection Act 2019
recommended_action: 1. Submit written complaint to the electricity board's consumer grievance cell immediately. 2. Request meter testing in writing. 3. Pay the average bill amount under protest to avoid disconnection. 4. If unresolved, file with CGRF. 5. Escalate to Electricity Ombudsman if needed.

title: Cheque Bounce — Recovery of Money
summary: Person's cheque bounced and issuer refuses to pay.
scenario: A person lent ₹2 lakhs to a friend who gave a post-dated cheque as repayment. The cheque bounced due to insufficient funds. The friend is now avoiding calls and refusing to repay. The lender wants to recover the money.
legal_guidance: A bounced cheque is a criminal offence under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act 1881. The payee must send a legal demand notice within 30 days of receiving the bank's dishonour memo. If payment is not made within 15 days of the notice, a criminal complaint can be filed in the Magistrate court. The offence is punishable with up to 2 years imprisonment or fine up to twice the cheque amount.
relevant_law: Negotiable Instruments Act 1881 Section 138, Section 142 (complaint procedure)
recommended_action: 1. Obtain the bank's dishonour memo immediately. 2. Send a legal demand notice within 30 days of dishonour. 3. If no payment in 15 days, file complaint under NI Act 138 in Magistrate court. 4. Attach original cheque, dishonour memo, demand notice, proof of delivery. 5. Also file civil suit for recovery if needed.

title: Bank Loan Recovery Agent Harassment
summary: Bank's recovery agent threatens and harasses loan defaulter.
scenario: A person defaulted on a personal loan EMI for 2 months due to job loss. The bank's recovery agent is calling at odd hours, visiting the workplace, and threatening the person and family members. The behaviour is abusive and intimidating.
legal_guidance: The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) has strict guidelines on loan recovery. Recovery agents cannot call before 7 AM or after 7 PM, cannot use abusive language, cannot visit without prior notice, and cannot harass family members. Violation of these guidelines can be reported to the RBI Banking Ombudsman.
relevant_law: RBI Guidelines on Fair Practices Code for Lenders, Consumer Protection Act 2019, IPC Section 503 (criminal intimidation)
recommended_action: 1. Record all calls and note dates/times of visits. 2. File complaint with the bank's grievance cell in writing. 3. File complaint with RBI Banking Ombudsman (bankingombudsman.rbi.org.in). 4. File police complaint under IPC 503 for criminal intimidation. 5. Seek legal advice on restructuring the loan.

title: Money Lent to Friend Not Returned
summary: Person seeks recovery of money lent to friend without written agreement.
scenario: A person lent ₹80,000 to a close friend over multiple occasions. There is no written agreement but there are WhatsApp messages acknowledging the debt. The friend has stopped responding and refuses to repay.
legal_guidance: Even without a formal written agreement, WhatsApp messages acknowledging the debt can serve as evidence under the Indian Evidence Act (Section 65B for electronic records). A civil suit for recovery of money can be filed in the Civil Court. For amounts up to ₹1 lakh, the case can be filed in the Small Causes Court or Magistrate court.
relevant_law: Indian Evidence Act Section 65B (electronic records), Code of Civil Procedure 1908, Limitation Act 1963 (3-year limitation for money recovery)
recommended_action: 1. Send a formal legal notice demanding repayment within 15 days. 2. Preserve all WhatsApp messages, bank transfer records. 3. File civil suit for money recovery in appropriate court. 4. File within 3 years of the last acknowledgment of debt. 5. Consider mediation as a faster alternative.

title: Credit Card Company Charging Illegal Interest
summary: Credit card company charges excessive undisclosed interest and fees.
scenario: A person's credit card outstanding of ₹15,000 has ballooned to ₹45,000 in 8 months due to compounding interest, late fees, and undisclosed charges. The bank never clearly disclosed these charges at the time of card issuance.
legal_guidance: Banks must disclose all charges under RBI's Fair Practices Code. Undisclosed charges are illegal. The consumer can file a complaint with the Banking Ombudsman for unfair charges. The RBI has capped credit card interest rates and mandated clear disclosure. Excessive charges can be challenged as unconscionable under the Consumer Protection Act.
relevant_law: RBI Master Circular on Credit Cards, Consumer Protection Act 2019, Banking Ombudsman Scheme 2006
recommended_action: 1. Request a detailed statement of all charges from the bank. 2. File complaint with bank's grievance cell. 3. If unresolved in 30 days, file with Banking Ombudsman. 4. File consumer complaint at District Commission for unfair trade practice. 5. Negotiate a one-time settlement if charges are disputed.

title: Employer Not Paying Salary for 3 Months
summary: Employee seeks recovery of unpaid salary from employer.
scenario: A private company employee has not received salary for 3 months. The employer keeps giving excuses about cash flow. The employee has been working regularly and has email records of salary promises. The total unpaid amount is ₹1.2 lakhs.
legal_guidance: Non-payment of wages is an offence under the Payment of Wages Act 1936. The employee can file a complaint with the Labour Commissioner. For establishments with more than 20 employees, the Industrial Disputes Act 1947 also applies. The employee can also file a civil suit for recovery of dues.
relevant_law: Payment of Wages Act 1936, Industrial Disputes Act 1947, Labour Commissioner jurisdiction
recommended_action: 1. Send a written demand notice to the employer. 2. File complaint with the Labour Commissioner or Labour Court. 3. Preserve salary slips, appointment letter, email records. 4. File complaint under Payment of Wages Act with the Authority. 5. Consider filing a civil suit for recovery if amount is significant.

title: Landlord Refuses to Return Security Deposit
summary: Tenant seeks return of security deposit after vacating rented property.
scenario: A tenant vacated a rented flat after 2 years and left it in good condition. The landlord is refusing to return the ₹60,000 security deposit claiming damage to property. The tenant has photos taken at the time of vacating showing no damage.
legal_guidance: A landlord cannot withhold security deposit without valid reason. The tenant can send a legal notice demanding return. If refused, a civil suit for recovery can be filed. In states with Rent Control Acts, the tenant can approach the Rent Controller. Photos and move-out inspection reports are strong evidence.
relevant_law: State Rent Control Acts, Transfer of Property Act 1882, Consumer Protection Act 2019 (if landlord is a business)
recommended_action: 1. Send a legal notice to landlord demanding deposit return within 15 days. 2. Attach photos, move-out inspection report, rent agreement. 3. File complaint with Rent Controller if applicable in your state. 4. File civil suit for recovery of deposit. 5. Claim interest on delayed return.

title: Neighbour Encroaches on Property Boundary
summary: Neighbour illegally constructs on shared boundary wall area.
scenario: A homeowner's neighbour has started construction that encroaches 2 feet into the homeowner's property. The neighbour claims the boundary is different. The homeowner has the original sale deed and survey map showing the correct boundary.
legal_guidance: Encroachment on private property is both a civil and criminal matter. The owner can file a civil suit for injunction to stop construction and for removal of encroachment. A police complaint can be filed under IPC Section 441 (criminal trespass). The local municipal authority can also be approached to stop illegal construction.
relevant_law: IPC Section 441 (criminal trespass), IPC Section 447, Code of Civil Procedure (injunction), Transfer of Property Act
recommended_action: 1. Get an official survey done by a licensed surveyor. 2. Send legal notice to neighbour to stop construction. 3. File complaint with local municipal authority. 4. File civil suit for permanent injunction and removal of encroachment. 5. File police complaint under IPC 441 if trespass continues.

title: Property Sold by Fraudulent Power of Attorney
summary: Person discovers their property was sold without their knowledge using fake POA.
scenario: A person living abroad discovered that their ancestral property in India was sold by someone using a fraudulent Power of Attorney in their name. The property has been sold to a third party. The person never gave any POA.
legal_guidance: Fraudulent use of Power of Attorney is a criminal offence under IPC Section 420 (cheating) and Section 467/468 (forgery). The sale can be challenged in civil court as void. A police FIR must be filed immediately. The sub-registrar who registered the fraudulent document can also be held accountable.
relevant_law: IPC Section 420 (cheating), IPC Section 467 (forgery of valuable security), IPC Section 468 (forgery for cheating), Registration Act 1908
recommended_action: 1. File FIR immediately at the police station where the property is located. 2. File civil suit to declare the sale deed void. 3. Apply for injunction to prevent further transfer. 4. Approach the Sub-Registrar to flag the fraudulent registration. 5. Engage a property lawyer urgently.

title: Flat Buyer Cheated by Unregistered Builder
summary: Builder collects money for flat but project has no RERA registration.
scenario: A person paid ₹15 lakhs to a builder for a flat under construction. The builder has no RERA registration and the project has stalled. The builder is not responding. The buyer has only a receipt and an informal agreement.
legal_guidance: Selling a flat without RERA registration is illegal under RERA Section 3. The buyer can file a complaint with the State RERA Authority. A police FIR can be filed under IPC 420 (cheating). The buyer can also approach the Consumer Commission. Recovery of money through civil suit is also an option.
relevant_law: RERA Section 3 (mandatory registration), IPC Section 420 (cheating), Consumer Protection Act 2019
recommended_action: 1. File complaint with State RERA Authority immediately. 2. File FIR under IPC 420 at local police station. 3. File consumer complaint at District Consumer Commission. 4. File civil suit for recovery of money paid. 5. Gather all payment receipts, messages, and informal agreement as evidence.

title: Tenant Refuses to Vacate After Lease Expiry
summary: Landlord seeks eviction of tenant who refuses to leave after lease ends.
scenario: A landlord's tenant's 11-month lease expired 6 months ago. The tenant refuses to vacate despite multiple requests and is not paying rent either. The landlord needs the property for personal use.
legal_guidance: After lease expiry, the tenant becomes a tenant at sufferance. The landlord can file an eviction suit in the Civil Court or approach the Rent Controller under the applicable State Rent Control Act. If the tenant is not paying rent, that is an additional ground for eviction. The process typically takes 6–18 months.
relevant_law: State Rent Control Acts, Transfer of Property Act 1882 Section 106 (notice to quit), Code of Civil Procedure
recommended_action: 1. Send a formal notice to quit under Transfer of Property Act Section 106 (15 days for monthly tenancy). 2. File eviction suit in Civil Court or Rent Controller. 3. File for recovery of unpaid rent simultaneously. 4. Do NOT forcibly evict — it is illegal. 5. Engage a property lawyer for the eviction process.

title: Wrongful Termination Without Notice
summary: Employee terminated without notice or severance pay seeks remedy.
scenario: A private sector employee with 5 years of service was terminated suddenly without any notice period or severance pay. The employer claims it was 'performance-based' but gave no written warning. The employee has a clean service record.
legal_guidance: Under the Industrial Disputes Act 1947, an employee with more than 1 year of continuous service cannot be terminated without following due process — written notice, inquiry, and retrenchment compensation. Wrongful termination can be challenged before the Labour Court. The employee is entitled to 15 days' wages per year of service as retrenchment compensation.
relevant_law: Industrial Disputes Act 1947 Section 25F (retrenchment compensation), Section 25G, Standing Orders Act
recommended_action: 1. Send a legal notice to employer demanding reinstatement or compensation. 2. File complaint with Labour Commissioner. 3. File case before Labour Court or Industrial Tribunal. 4. Claim retrenchment compensation (15 days per year of service). 5. Preserve appointment letter, salary slips, performance records.

title: Employer Deducting PF But Not Depositing
summary: Employer deducts PF from salary but does not deposit with EPFO.
scenario: An employee discovered that although PF is being deducted from their salary every month, the employer has not been depositing it with the EPFO for the past 18 months. The employee's PF passbook shows no credits.
legal_guidance: Non-deposit of PF contributions is a criminal offence under the Employees' Provident Funds and Miscellaneous Provisions Act 1952. The employer can be prosecuted and penalised. The employee can file a complaint with the Regional PF Commissioner. The EPFO can attach the employer's assets to recover dues.
relevant_law: EPF & MP Act 1952 Section 14 (penalties), Section 7A (determination of dues)
recommended_action: 1. File complaint with the Regional PF Commissioner (EPFO) immediately. 2. Provide salary slips showing PF deduction and PF passbook showing no credit. 3. EPFO will conduct an inquiry and can prosecute the employer. 4. File complaint with Labour Commissioner as well. 5. Consider filing police complaint for criminal breach of trust.

title: Sexual Harassment at Workplace
summary: Female employee faces sexual harassment from senior colleague.
scenario: A female employee has been facing repeated sexual comments and unwanted physical contact from her direct manager. She reported it verbally to HR but no action was taken. She is afraid of losing her job if she escalates.
legal_guidance: The Sexual Harassment of Women at Workplace (Prevention, Prohibition and Redressal) Act 2013 (POSH Act) mandates every employer with 10+ employees to have an Internal Complaints Committee (ICC). The employee can file a written complaint with the ICC within 3 months of the incident. If no ICC exists, she can approach the Local Complaints Committee (LCC) at the district level.
relevant_law: POSH Act 2013, IPC Section 354A (sexual harassment), IPC Section 509 (word/gesture to insult modesty)
recommended_action: 1. File written complaint with the company's Internal Complaints Committee (ICC). 2. If no ICC exists, file with Local Complaints Committee at district level. 3. Preserve evidence — messages, emails, witness names. 4. File police complaint under IPC 354A if needed. 5. Complaint must be filed within 3 months of incident.

title: Company Refuses to Give Experience Certificate
summary: Employer withholds experience certificate after employee resigns.
scenario: An employee resigned from a company after serving the full notice period. The company is refusing to issue an experience certificate and relieving letter, which is affecting the employee's new job offer. The employer claims the employee owes money which is disputed.
legal_guidance: An employer cannot withhold a relieving letter or experience certificate as leverage for disputed dues. This can be challenged as an unfair labour practice. The employee can file a complaint with the Labour Commissioner. A civil suit for mandatory injunction can also be filed to compel issuance of the documents.
relevant_law: Industrial Disputes Act 1947 (unfair labour practices), Contract Act 1872
recommended_action: 1. Send a legal notice to the employer demanding documents within 7 days. 2. File complaint with Labour Commissioner for unfair labour practice. 3. File civil suit for mandatory injunction if needed. 4. Inform the new employer about the situation with documentation. 5. Dispute the alleged dues separately.

title: Contract Employee Denied Permanent Status Despite Years of Service
summary: Long-serving contract employee seeks regularisation of employment.
scenario: A person has been working as a contract employee for a government department for 8 years, doing the same work as permanent employees. Despite multiple representations, the department refuses to regularise their employment. They are paid less than permanent employees for the same work.
legal_guidance: The Supreme Court in Uma Devi v. State of Karnataka (2006) held that regularisation is not an automatic right but courts have ordered it in cases of long continuous service. The principle of 'equal pay for equal work' under Article 39(d) of the Constitution applies. A writ petition in the High Court is the appropriate remedy.
relevant_law: Constitution of India Article 14 (equality), Article 39(d) (equal pay for equal work), Industrial Disputes Act 1947
recommended_action: 1. File a representation to the department head in writing. 2. If no response, file a writ petition in the High Court. 3. Gather evidence of continuous service, same duties as permanent staff, pay disparity. 4. Cite Supreme Court judgments on equal pay for equal work. 5. Engage a labour lawyer.

title: Online Financial Fraud — UPI Scam
summary: Person loses money in UPI fraud and seeks recovery.
scenario: A person received a call from someone claiming to be a bank official. They were asked to share an OTP to 'verify' their account. ₹45,000 was debited from their account immediately. The person realised it was a fraud and wants to recover the money.
legal_guidance: This is a cyber fraud under IT Act Section 66C (identity theft) and Section 66D (cheating by impersonation). The victim must report to the National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal (cybercrime.gov.in) and the bank immediately. Banks are required to reverse fraudulent transactions if reported within 3 days under RBI guidelines.
relevant_law: IT Act 2000 Section 66C, Section 66D, IPC Section 420 (cheating), RBI Circular on Limiting Liability of Customers
recommended_action: 1. Call bank immediately and block the account/card. 2. Report on cybercrime.gov.in within 24 hours. 3. File FIR at local cyber crime police station. 4. Send written complaint to bank demanding reversal under RBI zero-liability guidelines. 5. Keep all transaction records and call logs.

title: Morphed Photos Used for Blackmail
summary: Person's photos morphed and used to extort money online.
scenario: A woman's photos were morphed with obscene images and shared on social media. The perpetrator is demanding money to take them down. The woman is in distress and does not know the person's identity.
legal_guidance: This is a serious cyber crime under IT Act Section 67 (publishing obscene material) and Section 67A. It also constitutes criminal intimidation under IPC 503 and extortion under IPC 383. The National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal has a specific section for reporting crimes against women. The police can trace the perpetrator through IP address.
relevant_law: IT Act 2000 Section 66E (privacy violation), Section 67 (obscene material), IPC Section 383 (extortion), IPC Section 503 (criminal intimidation)
recommended_action: 1. Do NOT pay the extortionist. 2. Report immediately on cybercrime.gov.in (Women/Child section). 3. File FIR at local cyber crime police station. 4. Report the content to the social media platform for removal. 5. Preserve screenshots of all communications as evidence.

title: Fake Online Job Offer Fraud
summary: Person pays money for fake job offer and gets cheated.
scenario: A person paid ₹25,000 to an online recruiter who promised a government job. After payment, the recruiter stopped responding. The job offer was fake. The person has WhatsApp chats and bank transfer records.
legal_guidance: This is cheating under IPC Section 420 and cyber fraud under IT Act Section 66D. The victim can file a complaint on cybercrime.gov.in and at the local police station. WhatsApp chats and bank transfer records are admissible as electronic evidence under Section 65B of the Evidence Act.
relevant_law: IPC Section 420 (cheating), IT Act Section 66D (cheating by impersonation), Indian Evidence Act Section 65B
recommended_action: 1. File complaint on cybercrime.gov.in. 2. File FIR at local police station under IPC 420. 3. Preserve all WhatsApp chats, payment receipts, job offer documents. 4. Get Section 65B certificate for electronic evidence. 5. File complaint with the bank for fraudulent transfer.

title: Social Media Account Hacked and Misused
summary: Person's social media account hacked and used to send fraudulent messages.
scenario: A person's Instagram account was hacked. The hacker is sending messages to the person's contacts asking for money, claiming to be the account owner in an emergency. Several contacts have already sent money. The person wants to recover their account and stop the fraud.
legal_guidance: Account hacking is an offence under IT Act Section 66 (computer related offences) and Section 43 (unauthorised access). The person should report to the platform immediately and to the cyber crime portal. Contacts who sent money can file separate fraud complaints.
relevant_law: IT Act 2000 Section 43 (unauthorised access), Section 66 (hacking), IPC Section 420
recommended_action: 1. Report account compromise to Instagram/platform immediately for recovery. 2. Post a public notice on other platforms warning contacts. 3. File complaint on cybercrime.gov.in. 4. File FIR at cyber crime police station. 5. Advise defrauded contacts to file separate complaints and approach their banks.

title: Online Loan App Harassment and Data Misuse
summary: Instant loan app harasses borrower and misuses phone contacts.
scenario: A person took a small loan from an online app. After missing one EMI, the app's agents started calling all phone contacts, sharing the person's photo with abusive messages, and threatening arrest. The person is being publicly shamed.
legal_guidance: This is a serious violation of RBI guidelines on digital lending, IT Act Section 66E (privacy violation), and IPC Section 503 (criminal intimidation). The RBI has banned such practices. The victim can file complaints with RBI, cybercrime portal, and police. The app can be reported to Google Play/App Store for removal.
relevant_law: IT Act Section 66E (privacy violation), IPC Section 503 (criminal intimidation), RBI Digital Lending Guidelines 2022
recommended_action: 1. File complaint on cybercrime.gov.in immediately. 2. File FIR at cyber crime police station under IT Act 66E and IPC 503. 3. File complaint with RBI (sachet.rbi.org.in) against the illegal lending app. 4. Report the app to Google Play Store. 5. Inform contacts that messages are fraudulent.

title: How to File an FIR When Police Refuse
summary: Person seeks guidance on filing FIR when police station refuses to register it.
scenario: A person was robbed at knifepoint and went to the police station to file an FIR. The police officer refused to register the FIR saying it was a 'minor matter'. The person does not know what to do next.
legal_guidance: Under Section 154 CrPC, the police are legally obligated to register an FIR for cognizable offences. Refusal to register an FIR is itself an offence. The person can send the complaint by post to the Superintendent of Police. If still refused, a complaint can be filed before the Magistrate under Section 156(3) CrPC who can direct the police to register the FIR.
relevant_law: CrPC Section 154 (FIR), CrPC Section 156(3) (Magistrate's power to direct investigation), IPC Section 390 (robbery)
recommended_action: 1. Write the complaint and send by registered post to the Superintendent of Police. 2. If no action, file complaint before the Judicial Magistrate under CrPC 156(3). 3. File complaint with the State Police Complaints Authority. 4. Approach the High Court if all else fails. 5. Keep copies of all written complaints.

title: Neighbour Threatening and Abusing Regularly
summary: Person seeks legal protection from threatening neighbour.
scenario: A person's neighbour regularly abuses, threatens, and intimidates them over a parking dispute. The neighbour has threatened physical harm twice. The person is afraid and wants legal protection.
legal_guidance: Threatening someone with harm is criminal intimidation under IPC Section 503, punishable with up to 2 years imprisonment. Abusive language in public is covered under IPC Section 504. The person can file an FIR and also apply for a restraining order from the Magistrate under CrPC Section 107 (security for keeping peace).
relevant_law: IPC Section 503 (criminal intimidation), IPC Section 504 (intentional insult), CrPC Section 107 (security for keeping peace)
recommended_action: 1. File FIR at local police station under IPC 503 and 504. 2. Apply to Magistrate under CrPC 107 for a peace bond/restraining order. 3. Document all incidents with dates, witnesses, and if possible video recordings. 4. Send a legal notice to the neighbour. 5. Approach the local Resident Welfare Association.

title: Theft of Valuables from Home
summary: Person's house was burgled and valuables stolen.
scenario: A person returned home to find their house broken into. Cash of ₹30,000, gold jewellery, and a laptop were stolen. The lock was broken. They want to file a police complaint and understand the process.
legal_guidance: House break-in and theft is covered under IPC Section 454 (lurking house trespass) and Section 380 (theft in dwelling house). The person must file an FIR immediately. The police are obligated to investigate. The person should also file an insurance claim if the items are insured.
relevant_law: IPC Section 380 (theft in dwelling house), IPC Section 454 (lurking house trespass), IPC Section 457
recommended_action: 1. File FIR at local police station immediately — do not delay. 2. Do not disturb the crime scene before police arrive. 3. List all stolen items with approximate values. 4. Inform neighbours and check CCTV footage. 5. File insurance claim with the list of stolen items and FIR copy.

title: Bail Application for Arrested Family Member
summary: Family seeks bail for member arrested in a criminal case.
scenario: A person's brother was arrested by police in connection with a fight that resulted in minor injuries. He has been in custody for 2 days. The family wants to get him released on bail as soon as possible.
legal_guidance: For bailable offences (minor injuries under IPC 323), bail can be obtained from the police station itself. For non-bailable offences, a bail application must be filed before the Magistrate. A lawyer should be engaged immediately. If bail is denied by the Magistrate, an application can be made to the Sessions Court and then the High Court.
relevant_law: CrPC Section 436 (bail in bailable offences), CrPC Section 437 (bail in non-bailable offences), CrPC Section 439 (Sessions Court bail)
recommended_action: 1. Engage a criminal lawyer immediately. 2. Determine if the offence is bailable or non-bailable. 3. For bailable offence, apply for bail at the police station. 4. For non-bailable, file bail application before Magistrate. 5. If denied, escalate to Sessions Court under CrPC 439.

title: Stalking and Harassment by Ex-Partner
summary: Person seeks legal protection from stalking by ex-partner.
scenario: A woman's ex-boyfriend has been following her, repeatedly calling and messaging despite being told to stop, and showing up at her workplace. She is scared for her safety. The behaviour has been going on for 2 months.
legal_guidance: Stalking is a criminal offence under IPC Section 354D (inserted by Criminal Law Amendment Act 2013), punishable with up to 3 years for first offence and 5 years for repeat offence. The woman can file an FIR and also apply for a restraining order. Cyberstalking (online harassment) is covered under IT Act Section 66A (though partially struck down, IPC provisions apply).
relevant_law: IPC Section 354D (stalking), IPC Section 509 (word/gesture to insult modesty), IT Act Section 66C
recommended_action: 1. File FIR at local police station under IPC 354D immediately. 2. Apply for a restraining order from the Magistrate. 3. Preserve all messages, call logs, and evidence of physical following. 4. Inform workplace security. 5. Contact Women Helpline 181 for immediate support.

title: Contractor Takes Advance and Disappears
summary: Home renovation contractor takes advance payment and abandons work.
scenario: A homeowner paid ₹1.5 lakhs advance to a contractor for home renovation. The contractor did 20% of the work and then stopped coming. He is not returning calls and has not refunded the advance. The homeowner has a written agreement.
legal_guidance: This is breach of contract under the Indian Contract Act 1872 and may also constitute cheating under IPC 420. The homeowner can file a civil suit for recovery of advance and damages. A consumer complaint can also be filed if the contractor is a registered business. A police complaint under IPC 420 is also an option.
relevant_law: Indian Contract Act 1872 Section 73 (compensation for breach), IPC Section 420 (cheating), Consumer Protection Act 2019
recommended_action: 1. Send a legal notice demanding refund or completion of work within 15 days. 2. File consumer complaint at District Consumer Commission. 3. File civil suit for recovery of advance and damages. 4. File police complaint under IPC 420 if contractor is untraceable. 5. Attach written agreement, payment receipts, photos of incomplete work.

title: Shopkeeper Sells Expired Products
summary: Consumer seeks action against shopkeeper for selling expired food items.
scenario: A person bought packaged food items from a local shop. At home, they noticed the products were past their expiry date. The shopkeeper refused to exchange or refund. One family member fell ill after consuming the product.
legal_guidance: Selling expired food products is an offence under the Food Safety and Standards Act 2006 (FSSAI). The shopkeeper can be penalised. A consumer complaint can be filed at the District Consumer Commission. If illness resulted, a case for compensation can be made. The FSSAI can be reported to for action against the shop's licence.
relevant_law: Food Safety and Standards Act 2006, Consumer Protection Act 2019, IPC Section 272 (adulteration of food)
recommended_action: 1. Preserve the expired products as evidence. 2. File complaint with local FSSAI officer. 3. File consumer complaint at District Consumer Commission. 4. Claim medical expenses and compensation. 5. File police complaint under IPC 272 if illness was caused.

title: Dry Cleaner Damages Expensive Clothing
summary: Dry cleaner ruins expensive saree and refuses compensation.
scenario: A person gave an expensive silk saree worth ₹8,000 to a dry cleaner. The saree was returned with a large stain and a tear. The dry cleaner denies responsibility and refuses to compensate.
legal_guidance: This is a clear case of deficiency in service under the Consumer Protection Act 2019. The dry cleaner is liable for damage caused to goods entrusted to them. The consumer can file a complaint at the District Consumer Commission claiming the value of the saree plus compensation for mental harassment.
relevant_law: Consumer Protection Act 2019, Indian Contract Act 1872 (bailment — Section 151)
recommended_action: 1. Send a legal notice to the dry cleaner demanding compensation. 2. Preserve the damaged saree as evidence. 3. Get a valuation certificate for the saree. 4. File consumer complaint at District Consumer Commission. 5. Claim full value of saree plus compensation.

title: Parking Space Dispute in Apartment Complex
summary: Resident's allocated parking space occupied by another resident.
scenario: A flat owner in an apartment complex has an allocated parking space as per their sale deed. Another resident has been parking in their space for 3 months despite repeated requests to stop. The housing society is not taking action.
legal_guidance: An allocated parking space mentioned in the sale deed is the legal right of the flat owner. Occupation of another's parking space can be challenged as trespass. The flat owner can approach the housing society, the local Registrar of Cooperative Societies, or file a civil suit for injunction.
relevant_law: Maharashtra Cooperative Societies Act (or relevant state act), Transfer of Property Act, IPC Section 441 (trespass)
recommended_action: 1. Send written complaint to the housing society committee. 2. If no action, file complaint with the Registrar of Cooperative Societies. 3. Send legal notice to the encroaching resident. 4. File civil suit for injunction if needed. 5. Approach the Consumer Forum if the society is deficient in service.

title: Wedding Venue Cancels Booking Without Refund
summary: Wedding venue cancels booking last minute and refuses to refund advance.
scenario: A family booked a wedding venue 6 months in advance and paid ₹2 lakhs as advance. One month before the wedding, the venue cancelled the booking citing 'double booking'. They are refusing to refund the advance and offering only a future date.
legal_guidance: This is a clear breach of contract and deficiency in service. The venue is liable to refund the full advance plus compensation for the inconvenience caused. A consumer complaint can be filed at the District Consumer Commission. The family can also claim additional damages for expenses incurred in finding an alternative venue.
relevant_law: Indian Contract Act 1872 Section 73 (compensation for breach), Consumer Protection Act 2019
recommended_action: 1. Send a legal notice demanding full refund within 7 days. 2. File consumer complaint at District Consumer Commission. 3. Claim advance amount + additional expenses + compensation for mental harassment. 4. Attach booking receipt, payment proof, cancellation communication. 5. Seek urgent hearing given the wedding date.

title: How to Make a Valid Will in India
summary: Person wants to know how to write a legally valid will.
scenario: A 65-year-old person wants to write a will to distribute their property among their children. They have a house, fixed deposits, and gold. They want to ensure the will is legally valid and cannot be challenged.
legal_guidance: Under the Indian Succession Act 1925, a will must be in writing, signed by the testator, and attested by at least two witnesses who are not beneficiaries. The will does not need to be registered but registration makes it harder to challenge. A Hindu can also make a will under the Hindu Succession Act 1956.
relevant_law: Indian Succession Act 1925 Section 63 (execution of will), Hindu Succession Act 1956, Registration Act 1908
recommended_action: 1. Write the will clearly mentioning all assets and beneficiaries. 2. Sign the will in the presence of two witnesses (not beneficiaries). 3. Both witnesses must sign the will. 4. Optionally register the will at the Sub-Registrar's office for added security. 5. Keep the original in a safe place and inform a trusted person of its location.

title: How to Get a Legal Heir Certificate
summary: Family needs legal heir certificate after death of breadwinner.
scenario: A man passed away without a will. His wife and two children need to access his bank accounts, transfer property, and claim his pension. They have been asked to produce a legal heir certificate.
legal_guidance: A Legal Heir Certificate is issued by the Tehsildar or Revenue Department. It establishes who the legal heirs of the deceased are. For bank accounts and property transfer, a Succession Certificate from the Civil Court may also be required. The process varies by state.
relevant_law: Indian Succession Act 1925 (Succession Certificate), Hindu Succession Act 1956
recommended_action: 1. Apply for Legal Heir Certificate at the Tehsildar/Revenue office with death certificate, identity proofs of heirs, and relationship proof. 2. For bank accounts above a threshold, apply for Succession Certificate in Civil Court. 3. For property transfer, apply for mutation at the local revenue office. 4. Process typically takes 30–60 days.

title: How to Get a Name Change in Official Documents
summary: Person wants to legally change their name and update all documents.
scenario: A person wants to change their name after marriage. They need to update their Aadhaar, PAN, passport, and bank accounts. They are unsure of the legal process.
legal_guidance: A name change requires publishing a gazette notification (Central or State Gazette). After publication, the person can update their name in all official documents. For marriage-related name change, a marriage certificate is usually sufficient for most documents without gazette notification.
relevant_law: Gazette Notification process, Aadhaar Act 2016, Passport Act 1967
recommended_action: 1. Execute an affidavit on stamp paper declaring the name change. 2. Publish the name change in the Official Gazette (can be done online). 3. Publish in two local newspapers (required for some documents). 4. Update Aadhaar at UIDAI centre with affidavit and gazette copy. 5. Update PAN, passport, bank accounts with gazette notification.

title: How to Register a Rent Agreement
summary: Landlord and tenant want to know if rent agreement needs registration.
scenario: A landlord and tenant have agreed on a monthly rent of ₹12,000 for a 2-year period. They want to know if they need to register the agreement and what the consequences of not registering are.
legal_guidance: Under the Registration Act 1908, a lease agreement for more than 11 months must be compulsorily registered. An unregistered agreement for more than 11 months is not admissible as evidence in court. Most landlords use 11-month agreements to avoid registration. A registered agreement provides stronger legal protection to both parties.
relevant_law: Registration Act 1908 Section 17 (compulsory registration), Transfer of Property Act 1882 Section 107
recommended_action: 1. For agreements up to 11 months, notarised agreement is sufficient. 2. For agreements beyond 11 months, register at the Sub-Registrar's office. 3. Registration requires stamp duty (varies by state) and registration fee. 4. Both parties must be present with identity proofs. 5. Registered agreement is admissible as evidence in court.

title: How to File an RTI Application
summary: Citizen wants to know how to file an RTI to get government information.
scenario: A person wants to know the status of their pending government application (ration card) that has been stuck for 6 months. They want to use the Right to Information Act to get information from the government office.
legal_guidance: Under the Right to Information Act 2005, any citizen can request information from a public authority. The application must be filed with the Public Information Officer (PIO) of the concerned department. The PIO must respond within 30 days. If not satisfied, a first appeal can be filed with the Appellate Authority, and then with the State/Central Information Commission.
relevant_law: Right to Information Act 2005 Section 6 (application), Section 7 (disposal of request), Section 19 (appeal)
recommended_action: 1. Write a simple application addressed to the PIO of the concerned department. 2. Pay ₹10 application fee (by postal order, DD, or online). 3. Clearly state the information required. 4. Submit by post or in person. 5. If no response in 30 days, file first appeal with Appellate Authority.

title: Senior Citizen Neglected by Children
summary: Elderly parent seeks maintenance from children who have abandoned them.
scenario: A 72-year-old widow is being neglected by her two sons who have taken over her property. They do not provide food, medical care, or basic necessities. She wants legal recourse to get maintenance and her property back.
legal_guidance: The Maintenance and Welfare of Parents and Senior Citizens Act 2007 provides for maintenance of senior citizens by their children. A Maintenance Tribunal can order children to pay up to ₹10,000 per month. The Act also provides for cancellation of property transfers made under coercion or undue influence.
relevant_law: Maintenance and Welfare of Parents and Senior Citizens Act 2007, Hindu Adoption and Maintenance Act 1956
recommended_action: 1. File application before the Sub-Divisional Magistrate (Maintenance Tribunal) under the Senior Citizens Act. 2. Apply for interim maintenance. 3. If property was transferred under coercion, apply for cancellation of transfer. 4. Contact the District Social Welfare Officer for support. 5. Call Senior Citizens Helpline: 14567.

title: Husband Has Second Marriage Without Divorce
summary: Wife discovers husband has secretly married another woman.
scenario: A woman discovered that her husband of 10 years has secretly married another woman without divorcing her. He has been living a double life. She wants to know her legal rights and how to take action.
legal_guidance: Bigamy is a criminal offence under IPC Section 494, punishable with up to 7 years imprisonment. The second marriage is void under the Hindu Marriage Act. The first wife can file a criminal complaint under IPC 494 and also file for divorce on grounds of cruelty. She is entitled to maintenance and a share in matrimonial property.
relevant_law: IPC Section 494 (bigamy), Hindu Marriage Act Section 11 (void marriages), Section 13 (divorce grounds)
recommended_action: 1. Gather evidence of the second marriage — photos, witnesses, marriage certificate. 2. File FIR under IPC 494 at local police station. 3. File divorce petition on grounds of cruelty and bigamy. 4. Claim maintenance under CrPC 125. 5. Claim share in matrimonial property.

title: Chit Fund Company Closes Without Paying Members
summary: Chit fund company shuts down and refuses to pay subscribers.
scenario: A person was a member of a chit fund and paid ₹2,000 per month for 2 years (total ₹48,000). The chit fund company suddenly closed and the promoters are untraceable. Several other members are in the same situation.
legal_guidance: Chit funds are regulated by the Chit Funds Act 1982 and must be registered with the state government. Fraudulent chit funds can be prosecuted under IPC 420 and the Prize Chits and Money Circulation Schemes (Banning) Act 1978. Victims should file a collective complaint with the police and the state Registrar of Chit Funds.
relevant_law: Chit Funds Act 1982, Prize Chits and Money Circulation Schemes (Banning) Act 1978, IPC Section 420
recommended_action: 1. File FIR at local police station under IPC 420. 2. File complaint with the state Registrar of Chit Funds. 3. Organise with other victims for a collective complaint. 4. File complaint with the Economic Offences Wing (EOW) of police. 5. Preserve all payment receipts and chit fund documents.

title: Society Refuses to Give NOC for Property Sale
summary: Housing society refuses to issue NOC blocking property sale.
scenario: A flat owner wants to sell their flat. The housing society is refusing to issue a No Objection Certificate (NOC) claiming the owner has unpaid maintenance dues of ₹15,000 which the owner disputes. The sale is being blocked.
legal_guidance: A housing society cannot unreasonably withhold an NOC. If the dues are genuinely disputed, the owner can approach the Registrar of Cooperative Societies. The society must follow due process before claiming dues. Unreasonable refusal of NOC can be challenged before the Cooperative Court.
relevant_law: Maharashtra Cooperative Societies Act 1960 (or relevant state act), Model Bye-Laws of Housing Societies
recommended_action: 1. Request a detailed statement of dues from the society in writing. 2. Dispute the dues formally in writing with evidence. 3. File complaint with the Registrar of Cooperative Societies. 4. Approach the Cooperative Court if needed. 5. Pay undisputed dues under protest to unblock the sale.

title: Employer Refuses to Pay Gratuity
summary: Employee denied gratuity after 7 years of service.
scenario: An employee completed 7 years of service at a private company and resigned. The company is refusing to pay gratuity claiming the employee resigned voluntarily and is not entitled. The employee's last drawn salary was ₹35,000 per month.
legal_guidance: Under the Payment of Gratuity Act 1972, an employee who has completed 5 or more years of continuous service is entitled to gratuity upon resignation, retirement, or termination. Voluntary resignation does not disqualify the employee. Gratuity = (Last drawn salary × 15 × years of service) / 26.
relevant_law: Payment of Gratuity Act 1972 Section 4 (payment of gratuity), Section 8 (recovery of gratuity)
recommended_action: 1. Send a written application to the employer for gratuity payment. 2. If refused, file application with the Controlling Authority (Labour Commissioner) under Section 7 of the Gratuity Act. 3. Calculate entitlement: (35,000 × 15 × 7) / 26 = ₹1,41,346. 4. The Controlling Authority can direct payment with interest. 5. Employer can be penalised for non-payment.

title: Received Fake Legal Notice via Email
summary: Person receives fake court notice by email demanding money.
scenario: A person received an email claiming to be from the 'Cyber Crime Court of India' saying they are accused of viewing illegal content online and must pay ₹25,000 to avoid arrest. The email looks official with logos and case numbers.
legal_guidance: This is a well-known cyber fraud called 'sextortion' or 'fake legal notice scam'. No court in India sends legal notices via email demanding immediate payment. The person should not pay anything. This should be reported to cybercrime.gov.in and the email provider.
relevant_law: IT Act 2000 Section 66D (cheating by impersonation), IPC Section 420 (cheating), IPC Section 468 (forgery)
recommended_action: 1. Do NOT pay any money. 2. Do NOT click any links in the email. 3. Report the email on cybercrime.gov.in. 4. Report the email as phishing to your email provider. 5. File FIR at local cyber crime police station if you have already paid.

title: Accused of Crime Not Committed — Seeking Bail
summary: Innocent person falsely accused in criminal case seeks bail and defence.
scenario: A person has been falsely implicated in a theft case by a rival. The police arrested them based on a complaint. The person has an alibi and witnesses. They want to get bail and clear their name.
legal_guidance: A person falsely accused has the right to bail (for bailable offences at the police station, for non-bailable offences before the Magistrate). The accused has the right to legal representation. An alibi defence must be raised at the earliest stage. A counter-complaint for false FIR can be filed under IPC 182 (false information to public servant).
relevant_law: CrPC Section 436/437 (bail), IPC Section 182 (false information), Constitution Article 22 (right to legal counsel)
recommended_action: 1. Engage a criminal lawyer immediately. 2. Apply for bail — bailable offence at police station, non-bailable before Magistrate. 3. Provide alibi evidence and witness names to the lawyer. 4. File counter-complaint under IPC 182 for false FIR. 5. Apply for anticipatory bail if not yet arrested.

title: School Refuses Refund of Fees After Withdrawal
summary: School refuses to refund fees after student withdraws admission.
scenario: A parent admitted their child to a private school and paid ₹80,000 as annual fees. Due to relocation, the child could not join. The school is refusing to refund any amount despite the child never attending a single class.
legal_guidance: The Supreme Court and various High Courts have held that schools cannot forfeit fees if a student withdraws before the academic year begins. The UGC/state education department guidelines also mandate refund of fees in such cases. A consumer complaint can be filed against the school.
relevant_law: Consumer Protection Act 2019, Supreme Court guidelines on fee refund, State Education Department circulars
recommended_action: 1. Send a legal notice to the school demanding refund. 2. File consumer complaint at District Consumer Commission. 3. File complaint with the State Education Department. 4. Cite Supreme Court judgments on fee refund. 5. Claim full fees plus interest and compensation.

title: How to Challenge a Traffic Challan
summary: Person wants to contest an incorrect traffic challan.
scenario: A person received a traffic challan for jumping a red light. They have dashcam footage showing they did not jump the signal. The challan was issued based on an automated camera that appears to have malfunctioned. They want to contest it.
legal_guidance: A traffic challan can be contested by filing a representation with the Traffic Police or appearing before the Motor Accidents Claims Tribunal. The person can submit dashcam footage as evidence. Under the Motor Vehicles Act 1988, the accused has the right to contest the challan in court.
relevant_law: Motor Vehicles Act 1988, Motor Vehicles (Amendment) Act 2019
recommended_action: 1. Do not pay the challan if you intend to contest it. 2. File a written representation with the Traffic Police Commissioner with dashcam footage. 3. If challan is through e-challan system, contest online on the e-challan portal. 4. Appear before the Traffic Magistrate on the hearing date. 5. Submit dashcam footage as evidence.

title: How to Get a Caste Certificate
summary: Person needs guidance on obtaining a caste certificate for government benefits.
scenario: A person belonging to a Scheduled Caste community needs a caste certificate to apply for a government job reservation. They have never obtained one before and are unsure of the process.
legal_guidance: A caste certificate is issued by the Tehsildar or Revenue Department based on the applicant's community and their parents' records. The process varies by state. The certificate is required for availing reservations in government jobs, education, and schemes. It must be obtained from the district where the person's family originally belongs.
relevant_law: Constitution of India Articles 15, 16 (reservation), Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act 1989
recommended_action: 1. Apply at the Tehsildar/Revenue office of your native district. 2. Submit application with identity proof, address proof, and parents' caste certificate or school records showing caste. 3. The Tehsildar will conduct an inquiry. 4. Certificate is typically issued within 30 days. 5. Can also apply online through state government portals.

title: Husband Denies Wife Access to Matrimonial Home
summary: Wife locked out of matrimonial home by husband seeks residence rights.
scenario: A woman was locked out of her matrimonial home by her husband during a domestic dispute. He has changed the locks. She has nowhere to go and her belongings are inside. She wants to re-enter her home legally.
legal_guidance: Under the Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005, a woman has the right to reside in the shared household regardless of whether she has any title or ownership. She can apply for a Residence Order from the Magistrate. The husband cannot evict her without a court order.
relevant_law: DV Act 2005 Section 17 (right to reside in shared household), Section 19 (residence orders)
recommended_action: 1. File an application for Residence Order before the Magistrate under DV Act 2005. 2. Apply for interim relief for immediate re-entry. 3. Contact the Protection Officer in your district. 4. Call Women Helpline 181 for immediate assistance. 5. File FIR if physical force was used to evict you.

title: In-Laws Harass Daughter-in-Law for Not Bearing a Son
summary: Woman harassed by in-laws for giving birth to daughters.
scenario: A woman has been subjected to constant mental harassment by her in-laws and husband for giving birth to two daughters. They make derogatory remarks, deny her food, and have threatened to bring another wife. She is mentally distressed.
legal_guidance: This constitutes mental cruelty and domestic violence under the DV Act 2005. Gender-based harassment is also covered under IPC 498A. The woman can seek protection orders, monetary relief, and counselling orders from the Magistrate. This also violates the Pre-Conception and Pre-Natal Diagnostic Techniques (PCPNDT) Act's spirit.
relevant_law: DV Act 2005, IPC Section 498A, Constitution Article 15 (gender equality)
recommended_action: 1. File DV complaint with the Protection Officer. 2. Apply for protection order and monetary relief from Magistrate. 3. File FIR under IPC 498A for mental cruelty. 4. Seek counselling orders for the family. 5. Contact NGOs working on women's rights for support.

title: Hospital Overcharges for Treatment
summary: Patient seeks refund of excessive hospital charges.
scenario: A patient was charged ₹3.5 lakhs for a surgery that should cost ₹1.5 lakhs as per government-notified rates. The hospital is a private one empanelled under a government health scheme. The patient was not informed of the charges beforehand.
legal_guidance: Hospitals empanelled under government schemes must charge as per notified rates. Overcharging is a deficiency in service under the Consumer Protection Act. The patient can file a complaint with the State Medical Council, the National Consumer Commission (for amounts above ₹1 crore), or the District Consumer Commission.
relevant_law: Consumer Protection Act 2019, Clinical Establishments Act 2010, State Medical Council regulations
recommended_action: 1. Obtain itemised bill from the hospital. 2. Compare with government-notified rates for the procedure. 3. File complaint with the State Medical Council. 4. File consumer complaint at District Consumer Commission. 5. File complaint with the health scheme authority (e.g., Ayushman Bharat).

title: Investment Scheme Fraud — Ponzi Scheme
summary: Person loses savings in fraudulent investment scheme promising high returns.
scenario: A person invested ₹3 lakhs in an investment scheme that promised 30% annual returns. After 6 months, the company stopped paying returns and the promoters are now untraceable. Several hundred people are affected.
legal_guidance: This is a Ponzi scheme, illegal under the Prize Chits and Money Circulation Schemes (Banning) Act 1978 and SEBI regulations. The Economic Offences Wing (EOW) of the police handles such cases. SEBI can be approached if the scheme involved securities. Victims should file a collective FIR.
relevant_law: Prize Chits and Money Circulation Schemes (Banning) Act 1978, SEBI Act 1992, IPC Section 420
recommended_action: 1. File FIR with the Economic Offences Wing (EOW) of the state police. 2. File complaint with SEBI if securities were involved. 3. File complaint with the state's financial intelligence unit. 4. Organise with other victims for a collective complaint. 5. Preserve all investment documents, receipts, and communications.

title: Employer Discriminates Based on Caste
summary: Employee faces caste-based discrimination at workplace.
scenario: A Dalit employee is being systematically discriminated against at their workplace — denied promotions, given inferior work, and subjected to casteist remarks by colleagues and supervisors. HR has not taken any action despite complaints.
legal_guidance: Caste-based discrimination in employment is prohibited under the Constitution and the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act 1989. Casteist remarks are a criminal offence under the SC/ST Act. The employee can file a complaint with the police and also approach the National Commission for Scheduled Castes.
relevant_law: SC/ST (Prevention of Atrocities) Act 1989, Constitution Article 17 (abolition of untouchability), Article 16 (equality in employment)
recommended_action: 1. Document all incidents of discrimination with dates and witnesses. 2. File complaint with the police under SC/ST Act. 3. File complaint with the National Commission for Scheduled Castes. 4. File complaint with the Labour Commissioner for employment discrimination. 5. Engage a lawyer specialising in SC/ST rights.

title: Defamatory Posts on Social Media
summary: Person seeks removal of defamatory content posted about them online.
scenario: A person's ex-colleague has been posting false and defamatory statements about them on LinkedIn and Twitter, affecting their professional reputation. The posts claim the person was fired for fraud, which is completely false.
legal_guidance: Defamation is both a civil wrong and a criminal offence under IPC Section 499/500. Online defamation is also covered under IT Act Section 66A (though partially struck down, IPC provisions apply). The person can file a criminal complaint and also a civil suit for damages. The platforms can be asked to remove the content.
relevant_law: IPC Section 499 (defamation), IPC Section 500 (punishment for defamation), IT Act 2000, Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines) Rules 2021
recommended_action: 1. Screenshot and preserve all defamatory posts. 2. Report the posts to LinkedIn/Twitter for removal under their community guidelines. 3. Send a legal notice to the poster demanding removal and apology. 4. File criminal complaint under IPC 499/500. 5. File civil suit for damages to reputation.

title: Acid Attack Victim Seeking Justice
summary: Acid attack victim seeks legal recourse and compensation.
scenario: A young woman was attacked with acid by a rejected suitor. She has been hospitalised with severe burns. The attacker is known to her. She wants to ensure maximum punishment and compensation.
legal_guidance: Acid attack is a specific offence under IPC Section 326A (voluntarily causing grievous hurt by acid), punishable with minimum 10 years imprisonment up to life. The victim is entitled to compensation under the Nirbhaya Fund and state victim compensation schemes. The attacker should be arrested immediately.
relevant_law: IPC Section 326A (acid attack), IPC Section 326B (attempt to throw acid), CrPC Section 357A (victim compensation)
recommended_action: 1. File FIR immediately under IPC 326A. 2. Ensure police arrest the accused promptly. 3. Apply for victim compensation under CrPC 357A and state scheme. 4. Contact the District Legal Services Authority (DLSA) for free legal aid. 5. Apply for interim compensation for medical treatment.

title: Mechanic Overcharges for Car Repair
summary: Car owner seeks refund after mechanic overcharges for repairs.
scenario: A person gave their car for servicing at a local garage. The mechanic charged ₹18,000 for repairs but the person later found out the actual cost of parts and labour should have been ₹6,000. The mechanic refuses to provide a detailed bill.
legal_guidance: Overcharging for services is an unfair trade practice under the Consumer Protection Act 2019. The consumer can file a complaint at the District Consumer Commission. The mechanic is also required to provide an itemised bill. Refusal to provide a bill is itself a violation.
relevant_law: Consumer Protection Act 2019 Section 2(47) (unfair trade practice), Section 35
recommended_action: 1. Demand an itemised bill in writing. 2. Get an independent estimate from another garage. 3. Send a legal notice to the mechanic. 4. File consumer complaint at District Consumer Commission. 5. Claim refund of excess amount plus compensation.

title: How to Apply for Free Legal Aid
summary: Poor person needs legal help but cannot afford a lawyer.
scenario: A daily wage worker has been falsely accused in a criminal case. He cannot afford a lawyer. He has heard about free legal aid but does not know how to access it.
legal_guidance: Under the Legal Services Authorities Act 1987, free legal aid is available to persons below the poverty line, women, children, SC/ST members, victims of disasters, and persons in custody. The District Legal Services Authority (DLSA) provides free lawyers. The person can apply at the DLSA office or through the court.
relevant_law: Legal Services Authorities Act 1987, Constitution Article 39A (equal justice and free legal aid)
recommended_action: 1. Visit the District Legal Services Authority (DLSA) office in your district. 2. Submit an application with income proof (BPL card or income certificate). 3. A lawyer will be assigned free of cost. 4. In criminal cases, the court itself can assign a lawyer if the accused cannot afford one. 5. Call National Legal Services Authority helpline: 15100.

title: Ancestral Property Dispute Among Siblings
summary: Siblings dispute over division of ancestral property after father's death.
scenario: A man died without a will leaving behind a house and agricultural land. He has three sons and two daughters. The sons want to exclude the daughters from the property. The daughters want their legal share.
legal_guidance: Under the Hindu Succession Act 1956 (as amended in 2005), daughters have equal rights in ancestral property as sons. The 2005 amendment gave daughters coparcenary rights. All five children are entitled to equal shares. The daughters can file a partition suit in Civil Court if the sons refuse to share.
relevant_law: Hindu Succession Act 1956 Section 6 (as amended 2005), Code of Civil Procedure (partition suit)
recommended_action: 1. Daughters should assert their rights in writing to the brothers. 2. If refused, file a partition suit in Civil Court. 3. Apply for interim injunction to prevent sale of property. 4. Gather evidence of family relationship — birth certificates, father's death certificate. 5. The court will order partition and each sibling gets equal share.
